pages.text: Ductility characteristics of ﬁber-reinforced-concrete beams reinforced with FRP rebars Huanzi Wang a, Abdeldjelil Belarbi b,⇑ a AECOM Technology, Oakland, CA, USA b Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Houston, TX, USA article info Article history: Received 21 May 2010 Received in revised form 22 September 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 28 December 2010 Keywords: Crack width Ductility Fiber-reinforced-polymers Flexure Fiber-reinforced-concrete Polypropylene ﬁber abstract To mitigate the corrosion problem caused by steel reinforcement, a study was initiated to develop a non- ferrous hybrid reinforcement system for concrete beams by incorporating continuous ﬁber-reinforced- polymer (FRP) rebar and ﬁber-reinforced-concrete (FRC) containing randomly distributed polypropylene ﬁbers. This paper describes the ﬂexural performance of this FRP/FRC hybrid reinforcement system as well as FRP/plain concrete beams that served as references. Test results showed that the crack widths of FRP/ FRC beams were smaller than those of FRP/plain concrete beams at the proposed service load. The com- pressive strains at the top ﬁber of concrete in FRP/FRC beams were larger than 0.004 due to the added polypropylene ﬁbers. In addition, the ductility indices evaluating the FRP reinforced members were dis- cussed. It is found that the ductility indices for all the tested beams were above the minimum require- ment of 4. The addition of ﬁbers improved the ﬂexural behavior by increasing the ductility level more than 30%, when compared to the companion beam. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Ductility is a structural design requirement in most design codes. In steel reinforced concrete (RC) structures, ductility is de- ﬁned as the ratio of post-yield deformation to yield deformation which usually comes from steel yield. Properly reinforced and dimensioned concrete members exhibit a good ductile behavior. Ductile structural members can provide many beneﬁts to the per- formance of a global structural system. The most important as- pect of ductility is a precaution of structural failure. Ductile structures can provide an advanced warning before failure, while little or no warning can be observed before failure for the brittle structures. Due to the linear elastic properties of the FRP bars up to failure, the traditional deﬁnition of ductility applied to the steel reinforced structures cannot be directly applied to the struc- tures reinforced with FRP reinforcement. Several methods such as the energy-based method or the deformation-based method have been proposed to calculate the ductility index for FRP reinforced structures [1,2]. In addition, a great deal of effort has been made to improve and deﬁne the ductility of beams reinforced with FRP rebars. There are three well-known approaches to date. The ﬁrst ap- proach is to use the hybrid FRP rebars. Pseudo-ductile materials are fabricated by combining two or more different FRP reinforc- ing materials to simulate the elastic–plastic behavior of the steel rebars. Harris et al. [3] tested beams reinforced with hybrid FRP reinforcing bars and found that the ductility index of these beams were close to that of the beams reinforced with steel. This method showed some success in the research studies but re- sulted in limited practical applications because of the compli- cated and costly manufacturing process of the hybrid rebars. The second approach to improve

2. Research signiﬁcance New materials and design methods have been extensively inves- tigated to make a corrosion-free RC structure. This research project was initiated to develop a nonferrous hybrid reinforcement system for concrete beams using continuous FRP rebars and FRC containing polypropylene ﬁbers. This kind of hybrid system is expected to mit- igate the corrosion problem caused by the steel reinforcement, while providing required strength and stiffness. Meanwhile, ductil- ity of the FRP reinforcing system should be scrutinized more. This study aims to contribute to the increase of knowledge to this area. 3. Experimental program A total number of 12 beams categorized as six testing groups were investigated. Each testing group is composed of two similar beams subjected to monotonic load- ing and repeated loading/unloading, respectively. The experimental variables in- cluded FRP rebar size (#4 vs. #8), rebar type (GFRP vs. CFRP), and plain concrete vs. FRC. 3.1. Materials 3.1.1. FRP rods Three types of commonly used FRP rods were adopted in this study; namely #8 (25 mm) glass ﬁber reinforced polymer (GFRP), #4 (13 mm) GFRP, and #4 (13 mm) carbon ﬁber reinforced polymer (CFRP) as shown in Fig. 1 . The surface of the GFRP rods is tightly wrapped with a helical ﬁber strand to create indentations along the rebar, and sand particles are added to the surface to enhance the bonding strength. The surface of the CFRP made of epoxy modiﬁed vinyl ester is very smooth as shown in Fig. 1 . The mechanical properties of FRP rods provided by the manufacturer are summarized in Table 1 . 3.1.2. Polypropylene ﬁber Currently, many ﬁber types are commercially available in the form of steel, glass, synthetic, and natural ﬁbers. In this study, polypropylene ﬁber was used in FRC. The ﬁbers were ﬁbrillated and available in 57 mm length. 3.1.3. Concrete For practical application ensuring good workability of the concrete, the volume fraction of ﬁber ( V f) of 0.5% was chosen for FRC. Since this study aimed to investi- gate the beneﬁcial effects of FRC on the structural performance, especially ductility, variation of ﬁber contents was not considered as an experimental parameter. The concrete strengths at testing date were 30 MPa and 48 MPa for FRC and plain con- crete, respectively. 3.2. Test specimens The beams were 178 mm wide, 229 mm high, and 2032 mm long. No. 3 steel U- shape stirrups with a spacing of 89 mm were used at both ends of the beams to avoid shear failure, while no stirrups were used in the testing regions to secure pure bending behavior. The concrete cover was 38 mm for all beams. All beams were de- signed to fail by concrete crushing, which was accomplished by using a reinforce- ment ratio greater than the balanced reinforcement ratio qb. For the comparison purpose, the beams were designed to have a similar ratio of qf/qbf. The specimen details are shown in Fig. 2 . The nomenclature of test specimens is as follows; the ﬁrst character, ‘‘P’’ or ‘‘F’’, represents the concrete type for plain concrete and FRC, respectively; the second character, ‘‘4’’ or ‘‘8’’, is the rebar size in English designation used as reinforcement; the third character, ‘‘C’’ or ‘‘G’’, represents the rebar type, CFRP or GFRP; and the last character represents the ﬁrst beam or the second beam in the testing group, accord- ing to loading condition. The details of the specimens are shown in Table 2 . 3.3. Test setup and procedures The beams were subjected to a four-point ﬂ

increments were taken up to the initiation of cracking and ten increments up to fail- ure. At the end of each load increment, the load was held constant, crack patterns were mapped, and near mid-span crack widths were recorded. Each testing parameter was investigated using two identical specimens, as shown in Table 2 . One beam was loaded monotonically to failure. The other beam was subjected to loading/unloading cycles at 40% and 80% of its capacity, to evalu- ate the residual deﬂection, residual crack width, as well as the energy absorption capacity. 4. Test results and discussions This section provides a summary of the overall ﬂexural behavior of the FRP/FRC hybrid system in terms of crack distribution, load– deﬂection response, relative slip between the rebar and concrete, cyclic loading effect on ﬂexural behavior, and strain distribution in concrete and reinforcement. Comparisons between FRP/plain concrete system and FRP/FRC system is also discussed. 4.1. Crack distribution Like traditional steel rebar reinforced concrete beams, vertical ﬂexural cracks developed ﬁrst at the pure bending regions. Then, the inclined shear cracks were induced with the increase of load. 4.1.1. Crack spacing Table 3 shows the average crack spacing at 40% and 80% of the ﬂexural capacity to investigate the crack distribution at different load levels. With the increase of load, the crack spacing slightly de- creased. The crack spacing was virtually the same in both plain concrete beams and FRC beams at 80% of ultimate load, while the crack spacing of the FRC beams was about 20% smaller than that of plain concrete at a moderate service load (about 40% of ulti- mate load). The ﬂexural cracking can be closely approximated by analyzing the behavior of a concrete prism containing reinforcement with the same centroid. Cracks initiate when the tensile stress in the con- crete exceeds the tensile strength of concrete, f 0 t . When this occurs, all the force in the prism is transferred to the rebar. Away from the crack, the concrete stress is gradually built up through the bond stress between the rebar and the concrete. When the stresses in the concrete are large enough and exceed the tensile strength of concrete f 0 t , a new crack forms. The above mechanism is demon- strated in Fig. 4 a. With the addition of ﬁbers, the mechanism of crack formation is slightly changed as shown in Fig. 4 b. Some tensile loads can be transferred across the cracks by the bridging of ﬁbers. Thereby, the stress in the concrete comes from not only the bond stress, but also the bridging of ﬁbers. With the contribution from the ﬁ- bers, less bond stress is needed to reach the same cracking stress. Consequently, the crack spacing is smaller in the FRC beams than in the plain concrete beams ( S2 < S1 as shown in Fig. 4). At high load levels, ﬁbers are pulled out, and the contribution from the bridging of ﬁbers is diminished. CEB-FIP Code : The CEB-FIP Code [9] expression for the average crack spacing for the steel reinforced concrete is in the following manner: Sm ¼ 50 þ 0:25k1k2 db qt ð1Þ where db is the bar diameter, qt, the effective reinforcement ra- tio = As/Act; the effective concrete area in tension Act is generally LVDT 5 LVDT 4 LVDT (6,7) Demac Gages LVDT(1,2,3) Load, P LVDT8 1829 mm 229 mm Fig. 3. Flexural beam test setup (a) crack formation in plain concrete beam and (b) crack formation in FRC beam. Table 3 Comparisons between experimental and predicted average crack spacing of t

Fiber effect on crack width : With the addition of ﬁbers, the crack widths slightly decreased at the same load level, especially at the service load, as shown in Fig. 5 and Table 4 . As shown in Table 3 , the crack spacings were smaller in the case of FRC beams as com- pared to the plain concrete beams at the service load. As discussed earlier, the crack spacing tended to decrease at the service load due to the contribution of the ﬁbers. Since the crack width is propor- tionally related to the crack spacing, the crack width is expected to be smaller in the FRC beams at the service load. 4.2. Load–deﬂection response Fig. 6 shows the typical experimental moment–deﬂection curve for the plain concrete beams and the FRC beams reinforced with different types of FRP rebars. With increasing moment, cracks oc- curred in the testing region when the moment exceeded the crack- ing moment, Mcr. Consequently, the ﬂexural stiffness of the beams was signiﬁcantly reduced. As expected, due to the linear-elastic behaviors of FRP rebars, the FRP reinforced beams showed no yielding. The curves went up almost linearly until the crushing of concrete. 4.2.1. Fiber effect on moment–deﬂection curves To compare the ﬂexural behaviors between plain concrete beams and FRC beams, all the load–deﬂection curves of the plain concrete beams were normalized based on the following rules: (1) The moment was divided by a coefﬁcient CM, deﬁned as CM ¼ MACI/C0 plain MACI/C0 FRC , where MACI-plain and MACI-FRC are theoretical ultimate capacities computed from ACI 440 recommendation; (2) The deﬂection was divided by a coefﬁcient C D, deﬁned as CD ¼ DACI/C0 plain DACI/C0 FRC , where DACI-plain and DACI-FRC are the theoretical deﬂections calcu- lated from ACI 440 recommendations for plain concrete beams and FRC beams at service load (40% of the ultimate load), respectively. Fig. 7 and Table 5 shows that the ultimate moment capacities and deﬂections were increased in the FRC beams regardless of the type of reinforcements. The plain concrete beams failed in a more brittle manner than the FRC beams. Once they reached their ultimate capacities, the top concrete surface crushed and the load dropped suddenly and violently. Although the FRC beams also failed by top concrete surface crushing, they showed a more duc- tile behavior than the plain concrete beams. 4.2.2. and interrupted at a value of ec = 0.0045, which was corresponding to the test results: average of ecu = 0.0045. As shown in Fig. 7 , the theoretical curves show a good agreement with the experimental results. 4.3. Relative slip between longitudinal rebar and concrete at ends No relative slips were observed for any test specimens. It means that the development length designed by the previous study [12] was reasonable for FRP bars, which were capable of developing the required forces. 4.4. Loading/unloading effect on the ﬂexural behavior No signiﬁcant differences were observed before and after load- ing and unloading cycles in the crack width, crack distribution, and deﬂection. In addition, the ﬂexural stiffness was not signiﬁcantly changed after cyclic loading, as shown in Fig. 8. The ﬂexural perfor- mance’s insensitive to the loading/unloading cycles is mainly due to the linear-elastic strain–stress relationship of the FRP reinforcement. 4.5. Strains in reinforcement and concrete Fig. 9 presents the measured mid-span strains in reinforcement and concrete vs. the applied moment. It shows that strains in the reinforcement increases almost linearly up to failure. Because all the test beams failed by concrete crushing rather than FRP rein- forcement rupture, all measured strains in the reinforcement at peak point were less than the ultimate tensile strains. The mea- sured strains at the peak points were 12,000, 12,000, and 8000 microstrains for beams reinforced with #4 CFRP, #4 GFRP, and #8 GFRP, while the ultimate strains of each rebar were 16,700, 16,900, and 13,500 microstrains, respectively. The differences of moment–strain curves between the plain concrete beams and the FRC beams were signiﬁcant. Improvement of ductility is also proved from this observation. The plain concrete beams failed by concrete crushing at the top surface with sudden drop of reinforcement strain. In contrast to the plain concrete beams, the strains in concrete and reinforcement in the FRC beams increased gradually after the peak point. Furthermore, the ultimate strain in concrete also increased, which is well recognized as the beneﬁt from the adding of ﬁbers [13]. Ultimate concrete strains in plain concrete beams ranged from 2700 microstrains to 3300 microstrains with an average of 2950 microstrains; while in the FRC beams, they ranged from 4000 microstrains to 5000 micro- strains with an average of 4500 microstrains. 5. Predictions of the ultimate ﬂexural capacity As shown in Table 2 , the reinforcement ratio, qf, for all the beams are greater than the balanced ratio, qbf, deﬁned as follows: qbf ¼ 0:85b1 f 0 c ffu /C18/C19 ecu ecu þ efu /C18/C19 ð9Þ where ecu = 0.003 as deﬁned by [14]. The ACI 440 predicts the moment capacity based on the follow- ing equations: Table 5 Comparison of ﬂexural capacity and ultimate deﬂection between FRC and plain concrete FRP reinforced beams. Mn ¼ qf f f 1 /C0 0:59 qf f f f 0 c /C18/C19 bd 2 ð10Þ ff ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ðEf ecuÞ2 4 þ 0:85b1f 0 c qf Ef ecu s /C0 0:5Ef ecu ! 6 ffu ð11Þ There are two possible ways that ﬁbers can increase the ﬂexural strength. The ﬁrst way is a participation of the ﬁbers for carrying some portion of tensile stresses as an auxiliary reinforcement. The second way is an improvement of concrete properties. How- ever, the contribution of ﬁbers in tensile strength is not as signiﬁ- cant as the improvement of concrete property due to: 1. The relatively lower tensile strength of polypropylene ﬁbers (less than 1/3) than that of the steel ﬁbers. 2. The low elastic modulus of polypropylene ﬁber (500–700 ksi), resulting in the strain at break is three orders of magnitude greater than the tensile strain at failure of the concrete. Hence, the con- crete will crack long before the ﬁber strength is approached. Thus, increase of the ﬂexural capacity by ﬁbers is mainly achieved from an improvement of concrete properties. As shown in Fig. 10 , concrete strains at the ultimate state in FRC beams are signiﬁcantly larger than the value of 0.003 recommended by ACI. As shown in Table 6 , when the value of 0.0035 is used for ecu, the theoretical predictions agree well with the test results. Thus, it is suggested to increase the ecu to take advantage of the added ﬁbers. 6. Ductility evaluation As mentioned earlier, since the traditional deﬁnition of ductility cannot be directly applied to concrete structures reinforced with FRP reinforcement, it is necessary to develop a new approach as well as a set of ductility indices for evaluating the ductility perfor- mance of FRP reinforced members. There has been considerable ef- fort and discussion about this issue during the last two decades. Accordingly, two main approaches have been widely used. 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 55 Strain (×10e-6 mm/mm) Moment (kN.m) 0 50 100 150 200 250 300 350 400 450 Moment (kips-in.) Cs ¼ Mu Me¼0:001 ð14Þ Cd ¼ Du De¼0:001 ð15Þ Cc ¼ wu we¼0:001 ð16Þ The ductility indices computed by the curvature factor, Cc, dem- onstrated a more consistent tendency than these computed by the deﬂection factor, Cd [16]. Therefore, the curvature factor is used in this study. Fig. 11 shows the typical moment–curvature relation- ship of tested beams. The ductility indices are summarized in Table 8 . 6.3. Discussions of ductility index As shown in Tables 7 and 8 , the ductility indices computed by two methods are quite different. The effect from the addition of ﬁbers on the ductility indices is much more pronounced in deformation-based method. A schematic load–deﬂection curve for a certain material rein- forced beam, as shown in Fig. 13 , is created to explain the effects of adding ﬁbers. With the addition of ﬁbers, the load capacity and deﬂection increase proportionally from P1t o P2 and D1t o D2. Although there are different ways to calculate the ductility in- dex, there is no doubt that ductility is the ability to absorb the inelastic energy without losing the loading capacity. Therefore, higher inelastic energy absorption for the same system may ensure higher ductility. From this standpoint, the addition of ﬁbers should signiﬁcantly improve the system’s ductility. However, Naaman and Jeong’s [1] ductility index based on the energy-based method did not show a signiﬁcant difference between the plain concrete and FRC beams. This tendency is due to the proportional increase of elastic and inelastic energy absorption resulting in same ratios of E t/Ee. Finally, the values of lE remain constant regardless the exis- tence of ﬁbers. It can be concluded that the energy-based method cannot efﬁciently take into account the beneﬁts gained from add- ing ﬁbers to concrete such as the increase of the ultimate moment capacity and the accompanying increase of the deﬂection. In con- trast to the energy-based method, the Jaeger’s [2] ductility index based on deformation-based method can independently reﬂect fac- tors such as the load capacity as well as the deformation effect on the ductility. Meanwhile, all ductility indices calculated by the Jaeger’s method were above the minimum requirement of 4 [2,17]. 7. Concluding remarks An extensive research program was initiated to investigate the feasibility of a steel-free hybrid reinforcement system for concrete beams by combining FRP reinforcement with polypropylene ﬁber- reinforced-concrete. This paper presents the ﬂexural performances of FRP/FRC hybrid reinforcement system as well as FRP/plain con- crete beams that served as references. Discussions regarding the ductility evaluation for the FRP reinforced members are also pro- vided. From the investigation of this study, the following conclu- sions can be drawn: 1. The beneﬁcial effect of FRC on the various aspects of structural behaviors of FRP reinforced concrete beams is pronounced. The crack widths of FRC beams were smaller than plain concrete beam, especially at service loads. The ultimate concrete strains measured in the FRC beams were larger than the plain concrete beams. For the design of FRC beams, it is suggested to increase the ultimate concrete strain to take advantage of the added ﬁbers. By improving the concrete properties, adding ﬁbers has been proved to be an effective way to enhance the ductility of FRP reinforced system. Based on the deformation-based approach, the ductility indices increased by more than 30% with the addition of polypro

the concrete area surrounding the tension reinforcement of depth equal to 2.5 times the distance from the tensile face of the concrete section to the centroid of the reinforcement, k1 = 0.8 for high-bond bars and 1.6 for plain bars and, k2 = 0.5 for bending and 1.0 for pure tension. The crack spacing estimated by the CEB-FIP model was shown in Table 3 . The predicted values do not show a clear trend when compared with the test data. The predictions underestimate the crack spacing for GFRP reinforced beams; while they overestimate the crack spacing for CFRP reinforced beams. ACI 440: The ACI 440 recommends the following equation for estimating the average crack spacing of the FRP reinforced member: Sm ¼ 2kb ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ d 2 c þ S2 4 s ð2Þ where S is the bar spacing, dc is the thickness of concrete cover mea- sured from extreme tension ﬁber to the center of the closest layer of longitudinal bars, and kb, the coefﬁcient that accounts for the degree of bond between the FRP bar and the surrounding concrete. ACI 440 suggests 1.4 for deformed FRP bars if kb is not experimentally known. As shown in Table 3 , the accuracy of estimate is highly depen- dent on the value of kb, and the estimation is on the conservative side when kb = 1.4. 4.1.2. Crack width During the tests, crack widths were measured using the Demic gage. Fig. 5 shows the relationships between the crack width and the applied moment. Several analytical models for calculating the crack width are discussed and compared with the test results. The current ACI 440 committee recommends the following equation to calculate the crack width of FRP reinforced member: w ¼ 2 ff Ef bkb ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ d 2 c þ S2 4 s ð3Þ where w is the crack width at tensile face of the beam, ff, the stress in the FRP reinforcement, and b is the coefﬁcient to converse crack width corresponding to the level of reinforcement to the tensile face of beam, Toutanji and Saaﬁ [10] reported that the crack width was a function of the reinforcement ratio in the form of following equation: w ¼ 200 Ef ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃqf p bff ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ dc A 3 p ð4Þ where qf is the reinforcing ratio, A, the effective tension area per bar, and Ef is the modulus of elasticity of the reinforcement CEB- FIP model predicts the crack width as follows: w ¼ bSmem ð5Þ where b = 1.3, em is the mean reinforcement strain allowing for ten- sion stiffening; em ¼ rs ½1 /C0 b1b2ðrsr =rsÞ2/C138 =Ef . rs is the stress in the tension reinforcement computed on the basis of a cracked section. rsr is the stress in the tension reinforcement computed on the basis of a cracked section under loading conditions that cause the ﬁrst crack, b1 = 1.0 for high-bond bars and 0.5 for plain bars; b2 = 1.0 for single short-term loading and 0.5 for sustained or cyclic loading. As shown in Fig. 5, the predictions by Toutanji et al. model show poor correlation with the experimental results. For the beams with low reinforcing ratios (for the CFRP beams, q = 0.67%), the model overestimates the crack widths. Vice versa, for the beams with high reinforcement ratios (#4 GFRP beams, q = 2.2%, and #8 GFRP beams, q = 3.3%), the model underestimates the crack widths. Therefore, it can be concluded that bond characteristics affect the crack width of FRP reinforced member rather than the reinforce- ment ratio. Similarly, the CEB-FIP model’s accuracy is also dependent on the reinforcement ratio, as shown in Eq. (5). For this study, CEB-FIP model can predict the crack width fairly well for the GFRP r
